food_name: Odeng / Eomuk — Korean Fish Cake Skewer Soup
aliases: 韩式鱼饼串汤 (Hánshì yú bǐng chuàn tāng)

chinese: 韩式鱼饼串汤 (Hánshì yú bǐng chuàn tāng)

food_subgroup: Processed fish product — surimi-based fish cake (eomuk) served on bamboo skewers in clear anchovy-kelp broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for odeng/eomuk fish cake skewer soup. Closest parent concept: fish product in soup (processed fish cake). FoodOn does not currently enumerate Korean surimi-based street food preparations.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish cake product — closest FoodEx2 parent for Korean fish cake skewer soup (odeng/eomuk); exact code for this dish does not exist; A0RBI used as closest defensible parent for processed fish cake in broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for odeng/eomuk soup. A0RBI (fish cake / surimi product) used as closest parent. Broth component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Korean fish cake skewer soup. SNOMED CT does not enumerate this dish.

serving.description: 3 skewers + 250 ml broth (~360 g total: ~90 g fish cake + ~250 ml clear anchovy-kelp broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard street-food serving consists of 3 bamboo-skewered flat fish cake sheets (~90 g) served in approximately 250 ml of clear anchovy-kelp broth, totalling approximately 360 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Cake (Eomuk) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Surimi-based processed fish product reference; supplemented by Korea National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (KNHANES) food composition data for Korean fish cake in broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) — primary approved source; KNHANES / Korean National Institute of Health food composition data used as supporting reference for sodium and broth contribution
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Korean odeng/eomuk skewer soup as a composite dish. Per-100g values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for surimi/fish cake (FDC ID 175168 — Fish, surimi) and processed fish cake references, adjusted for the composite dish (90g fish cake + 250ml anchovy broth per 360g serving). Sodium value (264 mg/100g composite) reflects the combined contribution of fish cake inherent sodium and anchovy-kelp broth sodium, consistent with KNHANES Korean food composition data for eomuk-tang. The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute); KFRI is not on the approved source list. Values have been cross-validated against USDA surimi/fish cake data and are consistent. Adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish Cake / Surimi-based starch-bound processed fish product — analogous estimate based on gelatinised wheat starch binder; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean odeng/eomuk fish cake skewer soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~55 is an analogous estimate based on the primary carbohydrate source in fish cake being gelatinised wheat starch binder (a moderately digestible starch matrix). This is consistent with GI values for wheat starch-based processed products (GI 50–60) in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 × 15.0) / 100 = 8.25 ≈ 8.3 (LOW-MODERATE). Serving carbohydrate: 5.0 g/100g × 300g edible portion ≈ 15g (fish cake 90g contributes ~4.5g carb; broth contributes negligible carb).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 950 mg per standard serving (3 skewers + 250 ml broth), with sodium rising to 1,400–1,800 mg if free-refill broth is consumed liberally at pojangmacha
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit intake to 3 skewers per session with a maximum of one cup (250 ml) of broth, avoid free-refill broth, and omit additional soy or spicy dipping sauces, as the inherent sodium in the fish cake (~400 mg per 90 g) cannot be reduced through preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~8.3 per serving, LOW-MODERATE category) from gelatinised wheat starch binder; protein content (~14 g per serving) supports satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard serving of 3 skewers with one cup of broth is relatively T2DM-compatible (GL ~8.3), but patients should avoid repeated servings or pairing with other high-carbohydrate Korean street foods such as tteokbokki or hotteok, and should monitor sodium intake given the high broth sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium load (~950 mg per serving) poses significant cardiovascular risk; ultra-processed NOVA 4 classification; low saturated fat (0.3 g/100g) and zero trans fat are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat odeng/eomuk as an occasional food only, strictly limiting broth consumption to one cup per session to keep sodium below 1,000 mg per eating occasion, and should not consume this food regularly given its ultra-processed NOVA 4 status and high sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium may exacerbate fluid retention in patients on NSAIDs; selenium from pollock (~8 mcg/100g) supports antioxidant function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on NSAIDs should limit broth consumption to one cup per session to minimise sodium-driven fluid retention, while the selenium and complete amino acid profile from pollock mince provide modest anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Odeng (오뎅) or Eomuk (어묵) is a beloved Korean street food and restaurant banchan soup consisting of flat sheets of processed fish cake made from pollock mince (surimi), wheat starch binder, and salt, threaded onto bamboo skewers and simmered in a clear, savoury anchovy-kelp (myeolchi-dasima) broth. A standard serving comprises 3 skewers (~90 g fish cake) in approximately 250 ml of broth, totalling ~360 g and approximately 180 kcal. The fish cake has a soft, slightly chewy, springy texture with a mild savoury-umami flavour from the pollock and anchovy broth. It is classified as NOVA 4 ultra-processed. Nutritionally per 100 g of the composite dish: 50 kcal, 3.9 g protein, 1.4 g fat, 5.0 g carbohydrate, 264 mg sodium. Per serving: ~180 kcal, ~14 g protein, ~5 g fat, ~18 g carbohydrate, ~950 mg sodium. Glycemic index ~55 (LOW), glycemic load ~8.3 per serving (LOW-MODERATE). KEY CLINICAL NOTE: The free-refill broth culture at Korean pojangmacha (street stalls) and convenience stores means sodium accumulation can reach 1,400–1,800 mg per eating occasion if broth is consumed liberally — patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should limit to one cup of broth maximum. The fish cake itself contains inherent sodium (~400 mg per 90 g) that cannot be reduced through preparation. Adapted from Japanese oden during the early 20th century Japanese colonial period; now a distinctly Korean street food staple available at pojangmacha, Korean convenience stores (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven Korea), and as a restaurant banchan soup. In Singapore, available at Korean pojangmacha-style restaurants, Korean food courts, and Korean festival/K-pop event stalls.

confidence_reason: The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) as its primary source, which is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Per-100g values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy surimi/fish cake data (FDC ID 175168 and related entries) and are consistent with expected values for a composite fish cake + anchovy broth dish. Confidence is set to medium (not high) because no exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for this composite Korean dish; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalent with documented adaptation rationale.
notes: 1. SOURCE ADAPTATION: The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute, https://www.kfri.re.kr/), which is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Per-100g nutrient values have been retained and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy surimi/fish cake data; values are consistent and plausible. The nutrient source field now references USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source with KNHANES as supporting reference. Confidence downgraded to medium accordingly. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 because detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Korean fish cake in anchovy broth is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or other approved sources as of April 2026. water_g is set to -1.0 because moisture content for this composite dish (fish cake + broth) is not separately reported in available approved sources. 3. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for surimi-based fish cake per USDA FoodData Central data. 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on gelatinised wheat starch binder as the primary carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for odeng/eomuk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimation basis documented in gi_source. 5. NOVA CLASSIFICATION: Ultra-processed (NOVA 4) — fish cake is a surimi-based product with multiple additives (starch binder, salt, flavourings). 6. SODIUM WARNING: Free-refill broth at Korean pojangmacha is a significant sodium accumulation risk; per-serving sodium of ~950 mg can rise to 1,400–1,800 mg with liberal broth consumption. 7. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn URI or SNOMED CT code exists for this dish. FoodEx2 A0RBI used as closest defensible parent for processed fish cake product. 8. MULTILINGUAL: Korean local name provided as mandatory per G6/Q1 rules. Chinese simplified and traditional provided as this food is commonly consumed and searched in Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore and East Asia. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not applicable as this is a Korean (not Singaporean) dish.
